skill.name: ssh-penetration-testing
skill.description: Comprehensive SSH security assessment skill for authorized penetration testing, including enumeration, credential testing, vulnerability scanning, and tunneling techniques.
skill.summary: Authorized SSH penetration testing workflow covering enumeration, brute-force, exploitation, and post-exploitation techniques.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill contains documentation and examples for authorized SSH penetration testing. All detected security patterns (external commands, network access, filesystem operations, Metasploit references) represent legitimate professional security tools and methodologies. The skill explicitly requires written authorization and is designed for security professionals conducting authorized assessments. Hardcoded IPs are private network examples (192.168.1.x), not actual targets. This is educational content for security testing, not executable attack code.

medium_findings.title: Penetration Testing Tool Documentation
medium_findings.description: Skill documents professional penetration testing tools (Hydra, Medusa, Metasploit, Nmap). These are legitimate security assessment tools when used with authorization. All examples are educational documentation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Clear documentation of standard pentest tools with explicit authorization requirements. Tools are widely used in professional security assessments.

medium_findings.title: Network Pivoting and Tunneling Examples
medium_findings.description: Documents SSH tunneling techniques (local, remote, dynamic port forwarding, SOCKS proxy). These are standard network administration and authorized pentest techniques for accessing internal networks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Port forwarding and SOCKS proxying are legitimate SSH features used by system administrators and security professionals for network access.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Example IP Addresses
low_findings.description: Uses private network IP ranges (192.168.1.x) for documentation examples. These are RFC 1918 private addresses, not public targets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All IPs are in 192.168.0.0/16 private range - standard for documentation examples, not actual targets.

low_findings.title: SSH Directory and Private Key References
low_findings.description: References to ~/.ssh/, private keys, and SSH configuration files are standard reconnaissance activities during authorized security assessments. Skill includes legal authorization requirements.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Checking for exposed SSH keys is a standard security audit practice. Explicitly requires authorization before use.

low_findings.title: Brute-Force Documentation
low_findings.description: Documents credential testing techniques using Hydra and Medusa. Includes legal warnings and rate-limiting evasion techniques. Standard for authorized password security assessments.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Credential testing is legitimate for authorized security audits. Skill includes legal disclaimers and technical limitations.

dangerous_patterns.title: System Command Execution Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: Skill documents shell commands for security testing (nmap, hydra, ssh, etc.). These are examples in documentation, not automated executable code. All commands require manual execution by authorized users.

dangerous_patterns.title: Post-Exploitation Activity Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: Documents activities after gaining SSH access (privilege checking, key discovery, persistence). These are standard penetration testing phases for authorized assessments, not unauthorized access instructions.

content.user_title: Test SSH Security with Professional Penetration Methods
content.value_statement: Security professionals need comprehensive SSH assessment capabilities to identify vulnerabilities before attackers exploit them. This skill provides authorized penetration testing workflows for enumerating SSH configurations, testing credential strength, exploiting known vulnerabilities, and documenting tunneling techniques used in real-world attacks.
actual_capabilities: Enumerate SSH service versions, configurations, and supported algorithms using Nmap scripts and ssh-audit
Perform credential testing with Hydra, Medusa, and Metasploit frameworks for authorized password security assessments
Identify weak SSH configurations including deprecated algorithms, weak ciphers, and insecure protocol versions
Document SSH tunneling and port forwarding techniques (local, remote, dynamic/SOCKS) for network pivoting assessments
Conduct post-exploitation reconnaissance including privilege checking, key discovery, and persistence mechanisms
Generate comprehensive security reports with enumeration findings, credential test results, and vulnerability assessments
limitations: Requires manual execution of all commands - this is documentation, not automated attack code
All activities require explicit written authorization from system owners before testing
Rate limiting and intrusion prevention systems may block credential testing attempts
Key-based authentication and two-factor authentication prevent password-based testing methods
Cannot bypass modern security controls without authorization and legitimate access credentials

use_cases.title: Authorized Security Assessment
use_cases.description: Penetration testers conducting authorized SSH security assessments for organizations. Use this skill to systematically enumerate SSH services, test credential strength, identify vulnerabilities, and document findings for security reports.

use_cases.title: Internal Security Audit
use_cases.description: Security teams auditing internal SSH infrastructure for compliance and vulnerabilities. Use this skill to assess SSH configuration hardening, test authentication mechanisms, and verify security controls across the enterprise.

use_cases.title: Security Research and Education
use_cases.description: Security researchers and students learning SSH penetration testing methodologies in controlled lab environments. Use this skill to understand attack techniques, practice with professional tools, and develop defensive strategies.

prompt_templates.title: Basic SSH Enumeration
prompt_templates.prompt: Scan the network 192.168.1.0/24 for SSH services and enumerate their configurations. Identify versions, algorithms, and any weak configurations.
prompt_templates.scenario: Initial reconnaissance phase of authorized SSH security assessment

prompt_templates.title: SSH Credential Testing
prompt_templates.prompt: Test SSH credentials for user 'admin' on target 192.168.1.100 using the password list at /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt with Hydra. Use rate limiting to avoid detection.
prompt_templates.scenario: Authorized password security assessment for known username

prompt_templates.title: SSH Vulnerability Assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a comprehensive SSH audit on target 192.168.1.100 using ssh-audit. Identify weak algorithms, deprecated ciphers, and known vulnerabilities. Provide hardening recommendations.

prompt_templates.title: SSH Tunneling and Pivoting
prompt_templates.prompt: Establish a SOCKS proxy through SSH at 192.168.1.100 and use it to scan the internal network 192.168.2.0/24. Document the tunneling setup and internal findings.
prompt_templates.scenario: Network pivoting and internal reconnaissance through compromised SSH access

output_examples.input: Enumerate SSH services on 192.168.1.0/24 and identify weak configurations
output_examples.output: SSH service enumeration completed on 192.168.1.0/24. Found 3 SSH services: 192.168.1.10:22 (OpenSSH 8.4), 192.168.1.20:22 (OpenSSH 7.4), 192.168.1.30:2222 (OpenSSH 8.2). Weak configurations identified: diffie-hellman-group1-sha1 key exchange, arcfour cipher support on host 192.168.1.20. Recommendations: Upgrade OpenSSH on 192.168.1.20, disable weak algorithms, implement key-based authentication.

output_examples.input: Test SSH credentials for admin user using Hydra with rockyou.txt
output_examples.output: Hydra SSH brute-force test completed for target 192.168.1.100, user: admin. Tested 14,344,321 passwords in 2 hours 15 minutes. No valid credentials found. Target appears to have strong password policy or rate limiting enabled. Recommendation: Verify account exists, consider key-based authentication testing, review authentication logs for lockout indicators.

output_examples.input: Perform ssh-audit on 192.168.1.100 and provide hardening recommendations
output_examples.output: SSH audit completed for 192.168.1.100:22. Issues found: (1) Weak key exchange: diffie-hellman-group1-sha1, diffie-hellman-group14-sha1; (2) Weak ciphers: 3des-cbc, arcfour; (3) Weak MACs: hmac-md5, hmac-sha1-96; (4) RSA host key less than 2048 bits. Hardening recommendations: Add 'KexAlgorithms curve25519-sha256@libssh.org,diffie-hellman-group-exchange-sha256' to sshd_config; Set 'Ciphers aes256-gcm@openssh.com,chacha20-poly1305@openssh.com'; Remove 'MACs hmac-md5' line; Upgrade host key to 4096-bit RSA or Ed25519.

best_practices: Always obtain written authorization before conducting any SSH penetration testing activities
Use private network IP ranges (192.168.x.x, 10.x.x.x, 172.16-31.x.x) for documentation and testing examples
Implement rate limiting in credential testing to avoid account lockouts and intrusion detection triggers
Document all findings thoroughly including timestamps, tools used, commands executed, and results obtained
anti_patterns: Never run penetration testing tools against systems without explicit written permission from owners
Avoid using default wordlists without filtering - remove common false positives and inappropriate content
Do not bypass rate limiting or intrusion detection systems without authorization - this may constitute unauthorized access
Never store or transmit discovered credentials insecurely - use encrypted storage and secure communication channels

faq.question: Is this skill safe to use?
faq.answer: Yes, this skill is safe for authorized security testing. It contains documentation and examples for legitimate penetration testing methodologies. All activities require explicit written authorization from system owners. The skill uses private network IP ranges (192.168.1.x) for examples and includes legal disclaimers.

faq.question: What tools do I need to use this skill?
faq.answer: Required tools include Nmap with SSH scripts, Hydra or Medusa for credential testing, ssh-audit for configuration analysis, Metasploit Framework, and Python with Paramiko library. These are standard penetration testing tools available in security distributions like Kali Linux.

faq.question: Can I automate attacks with this skill?
faq.answer: No, this skill provides documentation and command examples, not automated attack code. All commands must be manually executed by authorized users. The skill requires human judgment and authorization for each testing phase.

faq.question: Why does the skill reference Metasploit and brute-force tools?
faq.answer: Metasploit, Hydra, and similar tools are industry-standard frameworks for authorized security testing. Security professionals use these tools to identify vulnerabilities before malicious actors exploit them. The skill documents their legitimate use in authorized assessments.

faq.question: What are the legal requirements for using this skill?
faq.answer: You must have explicit written authorization from system owners before conducting any testing. Unauthorized access to computer systems is illegal. Document all testing activities, stay within authorized scope, and follow responsible disclosure practices for any vulnerabilities found.

faq.question: Can this skill bypass SSH security controls?
faq.answer: This skill documents testing methods for identifying weak SSH configurations and credentials. It cannot bypass properly implemented security controls like key-based authentication, two-factor authentication, or modern intrusion prevention systems. Its purpose is to help assess and improve security posture, not bypass controls.
